title: Location Markers, Density Measurement, and Film Artifacts
narrationScript: The last pieces of proof a radiograph owes you are coverage, density, and honesty. Coverage means the film really saw every inch of weld it claims to have seen. Density means the image sits in the range where its contrast lives. Honesty means every mark on the film is either the part, or is identified as something else. Those three are location markers, density measurement, and film artifacts, and together they finish the verification you started in the last lesson. Location markers first. Markers are lead numbers and letters placed on the part at known positions so their images print on the film. They do two jobs. They map the film back to the part, and they prove complete coverage. The mapping side is plain discipline. Marker positions are marked on the part itself with a punch mark, paint, or another permanent reference, and they are recorded on the technique sketch. On a pipe weld the crew sets a zero point and a direction convention, so that years later a repair crew can find station three on the actual steel. The coverage side is where interpreters earn their pay. On any weld longer than one film, consecutive films must share markers. The same marker image appears at the end of one film and again at the start of the next. That overlap is the proof that nothing between the two films went unexamined. If two abutting films show no common marker, the length between them is unproven, and unproven means not examined. Placement has rules as well, because on curved parts a marker placed on the wrong side of the wall can appear to prove coverage that is not real. The codes spell out which side markers belong on for each geometry. What you confirm at the bench is simple. The markers are present, they match the sketch, they stay clear of the weld image, and every film overlaps its neighbors. Density next. Your eye is a terrible densitometer, so we use a real one. A transmission densitometer shines a small spot of light through the film and reads the density directly. Before you trust it, it gets checked against a calibrated step tablet, a film strip carrying steps of known density that traces back to a national standard. Shops typically verify the instrument at the start of each shift and expect agreement within about five hundredths of a density unit. Then you read the right places. Take a reading through the body of the hole type indicator, and take several readings through the area of interest along the weld. The single film minimums are typically one point eight for X ray work and two point zero for gamma, with a maximum of four for anything you view. And there is a window rule. The density anywhere in the area of interest must stay within minus fifteen percent to plus thirty percent of the density through the indicator body. Run an example. The indicator body reads two point four. Eighty five percent of that is about two point zero four, and one hundred thirty percent is three point one two. Any spot on the weld outside that band is not represented by that indicator, and the fix is another indicator covering the odd area, or another exposure. Artifacts last. An artifact is any image that did not come from the part, and they are born at every stage of a film's life. Before processing, a bent film leaves crescent shaped crimp marks, light if the bend came before exposure, dark if it came after. Sliding films apart in dry air leaves static marks, jagged black branching lines like lightning. Dirt trapped between the screen and the film leaves sharp light specks. During processing, chemistry leaves streaks and runback marks, drying water leaves round spots, fixer splashed on the film before development leaves light spots, and poor washing comes back months later as a yellow brown stain. After processing come scratches from rough handling. And from the exposure itself, a light image of the letter B on the film means backscatter came through the back of the cassette, and that film needs more backing lead and a reshoot. The discipline is what separates a Level Two from a film handler. Tilt the film under reflected light. Most artifacts live on the emulsion surface and show up as relief or sheen, while a real indication is a density image inside the film with no surface texture. Use the second film of a double load. A real indication prints at the identical spot on both films, and an artifact almost never does. Check the part when a surface condition could explain the image, because spatter and grinding marks are real objects that print honestly. If an artifact hides any part of the area of interest, the film is not interpretable there, and it gets reshot. Never call a discontinuity until the artifacts are ruled out, and never explain away a real indication as an artifact to pass a film. The film is evidence. Keep it honest.
imagePrompt: Gloved hands press a densitometer head onto a weld radiograph atop a light table in a dark film-reading lab, numbered lead location markers and a magnifying loupe resting at the edge of the glow.

sections.heading: Location Markers Prove Coverage
sections.body:
Location markers are **lead numbers and letters placed on the part** at known positions so their images expose onto the film. They do two jobs: they **map the film back to the part**, and they **prove complete coverage**.

The mapping is discipline. Marker positions are permanently referenced on the part - punch marks, paint, a stamped band - and recorded on the technique sketch. Pipe welds get a **zero point and a direction convention** so that a film calling out station 3 leads a repair crew to one unique spot on the steel, years later.

Coverage is where the interpreter earns the title. On any weld longer than one film, **consecutive films must share marker images**: the marker at the end of one film appears again at the start of the next. That overlap is the proof that the length between films was examined. **No common marker between abutting films means an unproven gap - and unproven means not examined.** Marker side matters too: on curved parts, codes specify whether markers go source side or film side for each geometry, because a marker on the wrong side can image inside the film edges while real coverage gaps hide beyond them. At the bench: markers present, matching the sketch, clear of the weld image, every film overlapping its neighbors.

sections.caption: Marker images shared by abutting films prove continuous coverage; films with no common marker leave an unproven length that must be reshot.

sections.heading: Measuring Film Density
sections.body:
The eye is a poor densitometer - adaptation, masking, and surrounding densities all skew its judgment - so density is **measured, not estimated**. A **transmission densitometer** pushes a small calibrated spot of light through the film and reads density directly. Before each use period - typically the start of each shift - it is verified against a **calibrated step tablet**: a film strip carrying steps of known density, traceable to a national standard. Agreement within about **plus or minus 0.05** density units is the usual expectation; a densitometer that cannot repeat that gets repaired, not trusted. Some procedures also permit comparison against calibrated **step-wedge comparison films** for a quick check.

Where you read matters as much as the instrument:

- **Through the body of the hole-type IQI** (between the holes, away from the ID numbers) - this is the reference density that the quality level was demonstrated at.
- **Through the area of interest** - several readings along the weld, including the lightest and darkest looking regions, not one polite reading in the middle.

Log the readings. Density values are examination records, and the numbers - not an opinion that the film 'looked dark enough' - are what the report stands on.

sections.heading: Density Limits and the Variation Window
sections.body:
Two layers of density rules apply, in the style of common codes such as ASME Section V.

First, the **absolute limits** from the viewing lesson: minimum **1.8** through the area of interest for X-ray exposures, **2.0** for gamma, maximum **4.0** for single or composite viewing - driven by where film contrast lives and what illuminators can penetrate.

Second, the **variation window** tied to the IQI: density anywhere in the area of interest must stay within **minus 15% to plus 30%** of the density measured through the IQI body. Work an example: the IQI body reads **2.4**. The floor is 2.4 x 0.85 = **2.04**; the ceiling is 2.4 x 1.30 = **3.12**. A weld region reading 1.9 or 3.3 is outside the window - the sensitivity demonstrated at the IQI does not represent that region.

When the window is exceeded, the fixes are procedural, not interpretive: place an **additional IQI covering the exceptional area**, or **reshoot** with a technique that holds the density band. And both layers apply together - a reading inside the window but below 1.8, or above 4.0, still fails the absolute limits.

sections.title: Numbers before opinions
sections.body: No interpretation happens until the measurements pass: densitometer **verified against the step tablet**, area-of-interest density **inside the absolute limits**, every reading **inside the minus 15% / plus 30% window** around the IQI body density, and the required **hole or wire visible**. A film that fails the numbers is not 'probably fine' - it is unverified, and unverified film proves nothing.

sections.heading: Film Artifacts: Where They Come From
sections.body:
An **artifact** is any image on the film that did not come from the part. They are born at every stage of the film's life, and knowing the life stage is how you recognize them.

- **Before processing:** bending leaves **crescent-shaped crimp marks** - *light* if bent before exposure, *dark* if bent after. Sliding films apart in dry air fires **static marks** - jagged, branched black lines like lightning. Grit between screen and film leaves **sharp light specks**; pressure from a dropped tool prints dark pressure marks; heat, age, or stray radiation lay down uniform **fog**.
- **During exposure:** light leaking into the cassette fogs the film edges black; a **light image of the lead letter B** taped to the cassette back means **backscatter** got through - add backing lead and reshoot.
- **In the processor:** developer streaks and runback trails from hangers, round **water spots**, *light* spots where fixer splashed the film before development, **reticulation** - a cracked-leather pattern from temperature shock - and regularly repeating roller marks from automatic processors.
- **After processing:** scratches from rough handling, and a **yellow-brown stain** that creeps in months later when fixing or washing was inadequate.

sections.caption: Quick recognition guide to common film artifacts seen at the viewing bench.
rows: Jagged, branched black lines or dot clusters
Films slid apart in dry conditions
Grit or lint between film and lead screen
Dark streaks trailing from hanger clip positions
Round light or dark spots with rings
Light image of the letter B
Scatter through the cassette back - reshoot with backing lead
Overall or edge density with no image detail
Bad storage, expired film, or light leaks

sections.caption: Simulated film panels showing the signature appearance of crimp marks, static discharge, processing streaks, and a backscatter B image.

sections.heading: Artifact or Indication? The Discipline
sections.body:
Every dark line on a weld image is a crack until proven otherwise - and every crack call is wrong if the line turns out to be an artifact. The verification habits:

- **Tilt the film under reflected light.** Most artifacts live on the emulsion surface and reveal themselves as relief, sheen, or scratch texture. A true indication is a density image *inside* the film - no surface character at all.
- **Compare the second film of a double load.** A real indication prints at the identical location on both films of the same exposure; an artifact almost never duplicates itself in register.
- **Check the part.** Spatter, grinding marks, and surface gouges are real objects that image honestly - look at the weld surface before crediting the film with finding something internal.
- **When in doubt, reshoot.** If an artifact obscures any portion of the area of interest, that portion is uninterpretable, and the film is rejected for that region.

Both failure directions are professional failures: calling an artifact a defect buys an unnecessary repair, and waving off a real indication as an artifact ships a flaw. Rule the artifacts out first, then interpret what remains.

interactive.title: Artifact or Indication?
interactive.intro: Each finding below turned up while viewing production film. Decide whether it is evidence about the part or a false image from film handling, processing, or the setup.
buckets: On the film or setup - false image

items.label: Sharp dark crescent on one film of a double-loaded cassette; the second film is clean at that spot
items.why: A dark crescent is a crimp mark from bending the film after exposure - and a real indication would print on both films of the same exposure.

items.label: Fine, wandering dark line at the identical position on both films of a double load
items.why: Duplication in register on both films means the image came through the beam - a fine wandering dark line is crack-like and belongs to the part.

items.label: Jagged, branched black lines like lightning radiating from one point
items.why: That is static discharge from sliding films apart in dry conditions - an emulsion exposure that has nothing to do with the part.

items.label: Rounded, sharp-edged dark spots clustered in the weld image, identical on both films
items.why: Rounded dark spots duplicated on both films are gas porosity in the weld - less metal in the beam path images darker.

items.label: A light image of the letter B near the center of the film
items.why: The lead B on the cassette back imaging light means backscatter reached the film from behind - a setup problem requiring backing lead and a reshoot, not a weld condition.

items.label: Sharp light spot in a GTAW root pass, present on both films
items.why: A duplicated light spot in a GTAW weld is a tungsten inclusion - denser than steel, it absorbs more beam and prints light.

items.label: Uniform dark streaks trailing down the film from the hanger clip positions
items.why: Streaks aligned with the hanger clips are processing runback - chemistry draining unevenly during manual processing.

items.label: Small sharp light specks; tilting the film under reflected light shows matching grit pressed into the emulsion
items.why: Dirt trapped between the screen and film blocks exposure and prints light specks - the reflected-light check confirms it sits on the surface, not in the image.

checkQuestions.question: Two abutting films of a long seam weld show no marker image in common. What has been demonstrated?
options: Complete coverage, since both films contain some markers
Nothing is wrong as long as each film meets density requirements
The length between the films is unproven - coverage is not demonstrated and the gap must be resolved or reshot
The weld is acceptable because no discontinuities appear near the film ends
checkQuestions.explanation: Coverage is proven only when consecutive films share a marker image at their abutting ends; without a common marker, the length between exposures may never have been examined.

checkQuestions.question: A densitometer is verified before use by:
options: Reading an unexposed sheet of film and adjusting to zero only
Comparing its readings against a calibrated step tablet traceable to a national standard, typically agreeing within about plus or minus 0.05
Measuring any production film twice and averaging
Visual comparison of the film against the illuminator at full power
checkQuestions.explanation: Densitometers are verified against calibrated step tablets of known densities, with agreement expected within roughly 0.05 density units; otherwise the instrument's readings cannot support an examination record.

checkQuestions.question: The density through the IQI body reads 2.0. Under the typical minus 15% / plus 30% rule, what density range is acceptable through the area of interest?
checkQuestions.explanation: 2.0 x 0.85 = 1.70 and 2.0 x 1.30 = 2.60, so area-of-interest readings must fall between 1.70 and 2.60 - and still satisfy the absolute single-film limits.

checkQuestions.question: A sharp dark crescent-shaped mark appears on a radiograph. This most likely means the film was:
options: Bent or kinked after exposure
Bent or kinked before exposure
Washed too long
Exposed through a cracked lead screen
checkQuestions.explanation: Crimp marks are crescent shaped: bending after exposure prints dark crescents, while bending before exposure prints light ones - a classic handling artifact pair worth memorizing.

checkQuestions.question: A light image of the lead letter B appears on a production film. The correct response is:
options: Accept the film - the B proves the cassette was assembled correctly
Reject the technique for backscatter: radiation reached the film through the cassette back, so add backing lead and reshoot
Interpret the weld normally since the B is outside the weld image
Increase film density on the next shot to hide the B
checkQuestions.explanation: The lead B on the cassette back is a backscatter detector: a light B image means scatter struck the film from behind, degrading image quality, so the setup needs additional backing lead and the exposure is repeated.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course 1.7-1.9
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (location markers, density limits, and backscatter check, general reference)
Calibrated step tablets traceable to national standards (densitometer verification, general practice)
